title: CLAUDE.md Editor for macOS — Wrangle
metaDescription: Edit CLAUDE.md files with a native macOS editor that understands AI project files. Token counting, XML highlighting, and embedded terminals. $19 one-time.
h1: the CLAUDE.md editor you've been missing
intro: CLAUDE.md is the file that shapes how Claude Code works in your project. It deserves better than a code editor's markdown preview pane.
body:
Every Claude Code project starts with a CLAUDE.md file. It defines your coding conventions, project structure, and instructions that Claude follows on every interaction. But editing it in VS Code means staring at raw markdown, no token count, and no sense of how the rendered output actually reads.

Wrangle gives CLAUDE.md files first-class treatment. The file tree recognizes them with a distinct icon. The editor renders your markdown inline — headers, lists, code blocks, all formatted as you type. The status bar shows a live token count so you know exactly how much context you're consuming before Claude even reads it.

If your CLAUDE.md contains XML blocks like <instructions> or <system>, Wrangle highlights them with proper syntax coloring and lets you collapse them to focus on the section you're editing. No more scrolling past walls of XML to find the line you need.

And because Wrangle has embedded terminals, you can edit your CLAUDE.md and immediately test it by running Claude Code in a tab right next to your editor. One app, one workflow.

title: Markdown Editor with Token Counting — Wrangle
metaDescription: A macOS markdown editor with always-visible token counting in the status bar. Know your context window usage before you send. $19 one-time purchase.
h1: markdown editor with built-in token counting
intro: When you're writing prompts and system instructions for AI models, every token matters. Wrangle shows you the count in real time.
body:
If you've ever hit a context window limit mid-conversation, you know the pain. You paste in your system prompt, your CLAUDE.md, your skill definitions — and suddenly you're over budget with no warning. Most markdown editors don't even know what a token is.

Wrangle puts a live token count in the status bar for every file you edit. As you type, the count updates. You always know exactly how much context your file will consume when fed to Claude, GPT, or any other model. No more copying text into a separate tokenizer tool.

This isn't just a nice-to-have — it changes how you write. You start trimming unnecessary instructions. You notice when a section balloons. You keep your prompts lean and effective because the feedback is always visible, not hidden behind a separate tool or API call.

Wrangle counts tokens using the same tokenization approach the models use, so the number you see is the number that matters. Combined with inline markdown rendering and XML block collapsing, you get a complete prompt-authoring environment in a native macOS app.

title: AI Prompt Editor for macOS — Wrangle

body:
AI prompt engineering has become a daily activity for thousands of developers. You're writing system prompts, few-shot examples, tool definitions wrapped in XML, and project instructions that shape how AI agents behave. But you're doing it in editors that were built for code or prose — not this weird hybrid that prompt files actually are.

Wrangle is a macOS-native markdown editor designed specifically for this workflow. It renders your markdown inline as you type (like Typora), but adds features no general-purpose editor has: XML tag highlighting for <tools>, <instructions>, and <system> blocks. Because it's a native Swift app — not Electron — it launches instantly, uses minimal memory, and feels like a real Mac app. Keyboard shortcuts work the way you expect. The app integrates with macOS notifications so your embedded terminal sessions can alert you when an agent finishes or needs input.

If you write prompts for Claude, GPT, Gemini, or any other model, Wrangle is the $19 tool that makes the process dramatically better.

Wrangle recognizes system prompt files (system-prompt.md, system_prompt.md) and gives them the attention they deserve. title: XML-in-Markdown Editor for AI Prompts — Wrangle
metaDescription: Edit markdown files with embedded XML blocks. Proper syntax highlighting and collapsible sections for <tools>, <instructions>, <system> tags. macOS native.
h1: finally, an editor that handles XML inside markdown
intro: AI prompt files mix markdown prose with XML-structured blocks. Every other editor chokes on this. Wrangle was designed for it.
body:
If you work with AI models, your prompt files probably look something like this: regular markdown for instructions, peppered with XML blocks like <tools>, <instructions>, <system>, and <examples>. It's a format that makes perfect sense for AI consumption but breaks every existing markdown editor.

VS Code treats the XML as invalid markdown syntax. Obsidian ignores it. Typora renders it as raw HTML. None of them give you what you actually need: proper syntax highlighting for the XML blocks, the ability to collapse them, and clean inline rendering of the surrounding markdown.

Wrangle handles this natively. XML blocks get distinct visual treatment — highlighted borders, syntax-colored tags, and one-click collapsing. The markdown around them renders inline as you type. You can focus on writing the prose instructions while keeping the XML structure visible but compact.

This isn't a plugin or an extension — it's built into the core editor. Every XML tag is recognized, styled, and collapsible out of the box. For developers who write AI prompts daily, this single feature eliminates a constant source of friction.

title: Claude Code Terminal for Mac — Embedded Agent Terminals — Wrangle
metaDescription: Run Claude Code sessions in embedded macOS terminals with smart notifications. Know when agents need input or finish tasks. Editor + terminal in one app.
h1: run Claude Code in embedded terminals on your Mac
intro: Stop switching between your editor and Terminal.app. Wrangle puts Claude Code sessions right next to your markdown files with smart notifications that pull you back when agents need you.
body:
Running Claude Code typically means opening Terminal.app or iTerm, navigating to your project, and starting a session. Then you switch to VS Code to check a file. Then back to the terminal. Then you forget which terminal tab has which project. Then you miss when Claude asks for permission because the tab was buried.

Wrangle eliminates this entire dance. Embedded tabbed terminals let you run multiple Claude Code sessions inside the same app where you edit your CLAUDE.md files. Each terminal tab shows its project context — the linked Claude file, active skills, and connected MCP servers — so you always know what's running where.

The real game-changer is smart notifications. When Claude Code needs permission to run a tool, needs your input, or finishes a task, Wrangle sends a native macOS notification. Click it and you're taken directly to the right terminal tab. No more polling. No more missed prompts.

You can run multiple agent sessions across different projects simultaneously. Switch between them with keyboard shortcuts. The terminal state persists — nothing gets lost when you move between tabs or projects.

title: AI Agent Notifications — Never Miss an Agent Prompt — Wrangle
metaDescription: Get native macOS notifications when AI agents need input, finish tasks, or require permissions. Stop polling terminals. Wrangle keeps you in the loop.
h1: never miss when an AI agent needs you
intro: You kicked off three Claude Code sessions and switched to another task. One of them needs permission. Another finished. A third is waiting for input. How long before you notice?
body:
The biggest friction in multi-agent workflows isn't the AI — it's the human. You start an agent, context-switch to something else, and then spend the next ten minutes wondering if it's done yet. You check one terminal — still running. Check another — it's been waiting for input for five minutes. The time you lose to terminal-polling adds up fast.

Wrangle solves this with native macOS notifications. Every embedded terminal session monitors for three key events: the agent needs permission to use a tool, the agent is waiting for your input, or the agent has completed its task. When any of these happen, you get an instant notification.

These aren't generic alerts — they're contextual. The notification tells you which project, which session, and what's needed. Click it and Wrangle brings you directly to that terminal tab, ready to respond. No hunting, no guessing.

This works across all your running sessions. You can confidently start multiple agents, switch to writing documentation or editing prompts, and trust that Wrangle will pull you back at exactly the right moment. It's the missing piece that makes multi-agent development actually practical.
features: One-click navigation to the right session

But Typora wasn't built for developers who work with AI agents. It doesn't understand CLAUDE.md or SKILL.md files. It renders XML tags as HTML instead of highlighting them. There's no token counter. And there's definitely no embedded terminal for running agent sessions.

Wrangle keeps what makes Typora great — the inline rendered editing experience — and builds on it for AI-native development. Your markdown renders as you type. Headers, bold, code blocks, lists — all formatted in real time. But now you also get XML block highlighting for <tools> and <instructions> tags. A live token counter in the status bar. File tree icons that distinguish between CLAUDE.md, SKILL.md, and AGENTS.md.

Plus embedded terminals with smart notifications, session context for every running agent, and the native macOS performance that comes from being a Swift app instead of Electron. If Typora is your baseline, Wrangle is Typora plus everything you need for AI development.

title: MCP Server Config Editor for macOS — Wrangle

h1: edit MCP configs with your agent sessions in view
intro: MCP servers extend what AI agents can do. Wrangle shows you which MCP servers are connected to each session and lets you edit their configs right next to the terminal.
body:
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is how AI agents connect to external tools — databases, APIs, file systems, custom services. Configuring MCP servers means editing JSON or markdown config files, then restarting your agent session to test. It's a cycle you repeat dozens of times as you dial in your setup.

Wrangle makes this cycle faster by putting everything in one place. The editor handles your MCP config files with the same inline rendering and syntax support it gives to all markdown and structured files. The embedded terminal runs your agent session right next to the editor. And the session context panel shows you exactly which MCP servers are connected to the current terminal.

This visibility changes how you work with MCP. Instead of guessing whether a server connected correctly, you can see it. Instead of switching between apps to edit a config and restart a session, you do it in two adjacent tabs. The feedback loop shrinks from minutes to seconds.

For developers building custom MCP servers or integrating third-party ones, Wrangle gives you the editing and testing environment that makes the iteration fast and the debugging visible.

title: Native macOS Markdown Editor — Swift, Not Electron — Wrangle
metaDescription: A true native macOS markdown editor built with Swift and SwiftUI. Fast launch, low memory, Apple Silicon optimized. Inline rendering and AI features. $19.
h1: a native macOS markdown editor — swift, not electron
intro: Every popular markdown editor is Electron. Wrangle is built with Swift and SwiftUI, runs on Apple Silicon natively, and uses a fraction of the memory.
body:
Open Obsidian, Typora, or almost any markdown editor and check Activity Monitor. You'll see Chromium helper processes eating hundreds of megabytes of RAM. That's Electron — a web browser pretending to be a desktop app. It works, but it's wasteful, and you can feel the difference.

Wrangle is built with Swift and SwiftUI, targeting macOS 15+ and Apple Silicon. It launches in under a second. Scrolling is smooth at any document size. Memory usage stays low because there's no embedded browser — just native macOS frameworks doing what they were designed to do.

This isn't just about performance snobbery. A native app means real macOS integration. Notifications work through the system notification center. Keyboard shortcuts follow Apple's Human Interface Guidelines. The app respects your system appearance settings. File dialogs are the real macOS file dialogs, not web recreations.

You also get all the features you'd expect from a modern markdown editor — inline rendering, XML block highlighting, token counting — plus developer-specific tools like embedded terminals, a fuzzy finder, and AI file awareness. All of it running natively on your Mac, the way apps should.

title: Context Window Management Tool for AI Developers — Wrangle
metaDescription: Manage your AI context window with live token counting, collapsible XML sections, and lean prompt editing. See token costs as you write. macOS native.

intro: Context windows have hard limits. Every token in your system prompt, CLAUDE.md, and skills is a token you can't use for conversation. Wrangle helps you see and manage these costs.
body:
Every AI model has a context window, and every file you feed it — system prompts, CLAUDE.md, skills, tool definitions — eats into that budget. Go over and you get truncated responses, lost context, or outright failures. But most developers have no visibility into how much context their files consume until it's too late.

Context window management becomes a natural part of your writing process, not an afterthought.

Combine this with collapsible XML sections and you get a powerful tool for auditing and trimming your prompt files. title: Obsidian Alternative for AI Developers — Wrangle
metaDescription: Like Obsidian but built for AI development. No graph view, no plugins — just fast markdown editing with token counting, XML support, and embedded terminals.
h1: an Obsidian alternative for developers who build with AI
intro: Obsidian is a great knowledge base. But if you're editing AI prompt files, skill definitions, and agent configs, you need something purpose-built — not a PKM tool.
body:
Some developers try to use Obsidian for their AI development files. It makes sense on the surface — Obsidian is a good markdown editor with a plugin ecosystem, and it works with plain files on disk. But Obsidian was designed for personal knowledge management, not AI development workflows.

Obsidian's strengths — graph view, backlinks, wiki-style linking — aren't useful when you're editing CLAUDE.md files and system prompts. Its weaknesses in this context are significant: no token counting, no XML block support, no embedded terminals, and Electron-based performance. The plugin ecosystem can partially address some gaps, but you end up duct-taping together an experience that still doesn't feel right.

Wrangle takes the opposite approach. Instead of being a general-purpose tool you customize, it's a purpose-built editor for AI development. Token counting is in the status bar, not a plugin. XML blocks are highlighted and collapsible natively. CLAUDE.md, SKILL.md, and AGENTS.md files get distinct treatment without configuration. Embedded terminals run your agent sessions inside the app.

And because Wrangle is built with Swift instead of Electron, it launches faster, uses less memory, and integrates with macOS natively — real notifications, real keyboard shortcuts, real file dialogs. If you're spending your days in AI prompt files, Wrangle is what Obsidian can't be.
features: AI file awareness out of the box
